attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a 12-month history of right knee pain which is worse at the end of the day and after exertion. Simple analgesia (paracetamol and/or ibuprofen) and supervised graded physiotherapy had initially helped but over the last three months have failed to alleviate the pain. He has also noticed his knee has become more stiff. He has no significant medical history other than obesity and hypertension. His only regular medications are amlodipine and paracetamol PRN.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. He has an antalgic gait with shortened stance phase and stride length on the right. His right knee has a palpable crepitus and painful range of motion (ROM). The left knee by comparison has limited but greater ROM that is not painful.

Investigations reveal a complete blood examination and C-reactive protein within normal limits. Bilateral knee X-rays show significant medial compartmental joint space narrowing with osteophytes and sclerotic changes in the right knee and mild medial compartmental joint space narrowing in the left knee.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate step for long-term management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptomatic right knee osteoarthritis that has failed to respond to conservative management. Given the isolated medial compartment involvement and the failure of non-surgical treatments, right knee medial unicompartmental arthroplasty is the most appropriate long-term management option. This procedure is indicated in symptomatic isolated unicompartmental disease where conservative treatment has failed to improve symptoms. It can provide significant pain relief and functional improvement while preserving more of the native knee anatomy compared to total knee arthroplasty. Additionally, unicompartmental arthroplasty is often suitable for patients with obesity, as it involves a less invasive approach and can lead to quicker recovery and less strain on the joint post-surgery.

Bilateral total knee arthroplasty would be an overly aggressive approach in this case. Although the patient has bilateral radiographic degenerative changes, he is asymptomatic on the left. Performing surgery on an asymptomatic knee is not indicated and carries unnecessary risks. Furthermore, in obese patients, bilateral total knee arthroplasty can be associated with increased perioperative risks and complications, making it less suitable.

Regular tramadol is not an appropriate long-term management strategy for osteoarthritis. There is evidence to show that oral narcotics such as tramadol result in an increased risk of significant adverse events and have limited effectiveness in improving pain and range of motion. Long-term opioid use also carries risks of dependence and other side effects.

Increasing the intensity of physiotherapy is unlikely to provide significant additional benefit at this stage. The patient has already undergone supervised graded physiotherapy, which was initially helpful but has failed to alleviate pain in recent months. While exercise remains an important component of osteoarthritis management, more intensive physiotherapy alone is unlikely to provide adequate long-term symptom relief in this case.

Intra-articular corticosteroid injection can provide short-term relief for symptomatic osteoarthritis but is not a practical long-term solution. These injections typically provide temporary relief and are often used as a bridging treatment. They are not recommended for frequent, repeated use due to potential adverse effects on joint cartilage.

It's worth noting that while unicompartmental knee arthroplasty is appropriate in this case, total knee arthroplasties generally have lower revision rates than unicompartmental knee arthroplasties in the setting of unicompartmental osteoarthritis. However, unicompartmental arthroplasty offers advantages such as faster recovery, better range of motion, and preservation of bone stock. The choice between these options would typically involve a detailed discussion with the patient about the risks and benefits of each approach.
learningPoints: Patient education, weight loss, over-the-counter analgesia and physiotherapy are first-line management options for patients with knee osteoarthritis.
Unicompartmental knee arthroplasty is indicated for symptomatic isolated unicompartmental osteoarthritis where conservative treatment has failed.
Total knee arthroplasty has lower revision rates than unicompartmental knee arthroplasty in the setting of unicompartmental osteoarthritis.
references: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons Management of Osteoarthritis of the Knee (Non Arthroplasty) Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline [online]. 2021. Available at: https://www.aaos.org/oak3cpg

McGrory BJ, Weber KL, Jevsevar DS, Sevarino K. Surgical Management of Osteoarthritis of the Knee: Evidence-based Guideline. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2016 Aug;24(8):e87-93. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27355286/

Aggarwal VA, Sambandam SN, Wukich DK. The impact of obesity on total knee arthroplasty outcomes: A retrospective matched cohort study. J Clin Orthop Trauma. 2022 Aug 20;33:101987. doi: 10.1016/j.jcot.2022.101987. PMID: 36089991; PMCID: PMC9449637.
